content.scenario: Security Operations Basics 🛡️: SIEM vs SOAR
content.text: While a SIEM aggregates and analyzes security log data, a {{c1::SOAR}} platform automates threat response workflows using playbooks.
content.explanation: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) collects and correlates logs. SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) takes actions automatically, e.g. blocking IPs or isolating hosts using APIs.
content.spanish: Mientras que un SIEM agrega y analiza datos de registros de seguridad, una plataforma SOAR automatiza los flujos de trabajo de respuesta a amenazas mediante playbooks.
content.usage:
SIEM: Splunk, Sentinel, ELK
SOAR: Splunk Phantom, Cortex XSOAR

content.text: Unlike stateless packet filtering which only checks headers individually, {{c1::stateful inspection}} firewalls track the state of active TCP connections to validate incoming traffic.
content.explanation: Stateful firewalls maintain a state table. If a packet matches an established outbound connection (e.g., in ESTABLISHED state), it is automatically allowed back in, reducing rule complexity.
content.spanish: A diferencia del filtrado de paquetes sin estado que solo verifica los encabezados individualmente, los firewalls de inspección con estado (stateful inspection) rastrean el estado de las conexiones TCP activas.
content.usage:
Stateless: ACLs based only on IP/Port
Stateful: Checks connection handshake state

content.text: A decoy server configured to look like a high-value vulnerable target to lure attackers and log their tactics is called a {{c1::honeypot}}.
content.explanation: S have no legitimate business traffic. Any interaction with them is immediately flagged as a high-fidelity security alert, indicating probing or compromise
content.spanish: Un servidor señuelo configurado para parecer un objetivo vulnerable de alto valor con el fin de atraer a los atacantes y registrar sus tácticas se denomina honeypot.

content.scenario: Security Operations Operations ⚙️: Alert Fatigue Tuning
content.text: To prevent analyst alert fatigue, engineers perform alert tuning by modifying rule thresholds to minimize {{c1::false positives}}.
content.explanation: A false positive is a legitimate system action flagged as malicious. High false positive rates clog incident queues, causing analysts to miss real attacks (false negatives).
content.spanish: Para evitar la fatiga por alertas de los analistas, los ingenieros realizan un ajuste de alertas modificando los umbrales de las reglas para minimizar los falsos positivos.
content.usage: Tuning practice: Tuning out DNS queries to known CDN servers from security scanners

content.text: The security practice of proactively searching networks to find malicious activities that have bypassed active security controls is called {{c1::Threat Hunting}}.
content.explanation: Is proactive. Instead of waiting for a SIEM alert, hunters formulate hypotheses based on threat intelligence and query historical logs for stealthy attacker behaviors
content.spanish: La práctica de seguridad de buscar proactivamente en las redes para encontrar actividades maliciosas que han eludido los controles de seguridad activos se denomina Threat Hunting.
content.usage: Trigger: Hypothesis creation based on new vulnerability disclosures

content.text: To prevent compromised internal servers from establishing outbound connections to malicious C2 servers, network admins implement {{c1::egress filtering}}.
content.explanation: Blocks outbound network traffic. By denying servers from connecting out to arbitrary external ports (allowing only specific destination IPs or HTTP proxies), exfiltration and reverse shells are blocked
content.spanish: Para evitar que los servidores internos comprometidos establezcan conexiones salientes con servidores C2 maliciosos, los administradores de red implementan el filtrado de salida (egress filtering).
content.usage: Rule: Deny all outbound traffic from database subnets to internet

content.text: The cybersecurity strategy of layering multiple defensive controls across network, host, and data levels is called {{c1::Defense-in-Depth}}.
content.explanation: No single security control is foolproof. If an attacker bypasses the network firewall, endpoint detection (EDR), user privilege limits, and file encryption still stand in their way.
content.spanish: La estrategia de ciberseguridad de organizar múltiples controles defensivos en capas a nivel de red, host y datos se denomina defensa en profundidad (Defense-in-Depth).
content.usage: Layers: Physical Security → Perimeter Firewall → EDR → Access control (IAM) → Data Encryption

content.scenario: Security Operations Engineering 🛠️: SIEM Alert Correlation
content.text:
Question: Which log correlation logic provides the highest fidelity indicator of a successful brute-force attack followed by compromised access?
Options:
  • A firewall deny rule followed by an administrative login
  • Multiple failed login events from an IP followed by a successful login event from that same IP
  • An outbound web connection followed immediately by a DNS TXT record query
  • Multiple successful logins from different internal subnets within the same hour

Correct Answer: {{c1::Multiple failed login events from an IP followed by a successful login event from that same IP}}
content.explanation: Brute-force attempts generate multiple failed login events (e.g. Windows Event ID 4625). If the sequence terminates with a successful login (Event ID 4624) from the same source IP, it indicates the attacker guessed the password.
content.spanish: ¿Qué lógica de correlación de registros proporciona el indicador más fiel de un ataque de fuerza bruta exitoso seguido de un acceso comprometido?
content.usage: • Brute-force signature: Event ID 4625 (Failed) followed by Event ID 4624 (Success) from the same Source IP.

content.scenario: Security Operations Engineering 🛠️: Active Defense Controls
content.text: Unlike an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) which only flags alerts, an {{c1::Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)}} sits in-line and drops matching traffic blocks dynamically.
content.explanation: IDS runs out-of-band (via network span ports) and cannot block traffic. IPS runs in-line (packets pass through it) allowing it to drop packets and terminate malicious TCP sessions.
content.spanish: A diferencia de un Sistema de Detección de Intrusos (IDS) que solo marca alertas, un Sistema de Prevención de Intrusos (IPS) se ubica en línea y descarta dinámicamente bloques de tráfico coincidentes.
content.usage:
IDS placement: TAP/SPAN network mirror ports
IPS placement: Directly between firewall and switch (in-line)

content.scenario: Security Operations Engineering 🛠️: Active Directory Hardening
content.text: To mitigate Active Directory Kerberoasting attacks (where service ticket hashes are dumped and cracked offline), administrators must configure accounts to use {{c1::AES-256 encryption}} and strong passwords.
content.explanation: Kerberoasting exploits weak RC4 encryption hashes returned by Active Directory when querying a Service Principal Name (SPN). Enforcing AES-256 and long passwords makes offline cracking practically impossible.
content.spanish: Para mitigar los ataques de Kerberoasting en Active Directory (donde los hashes de tickets de servicio se extraen y se descifran sin conexión), los administradores deben configurar las cuentas para usar cifrado AES-256.
content.usage: Remediation: 1. Set Service Account passwords to 25+ characters, 2. Enforce AES Kerberos encryption
